If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our community forum where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Service Incident - June 11, 2026 - AI Agents | All Pods - AIAA: Technical errors in AI agent conversations causing loss of access to AIAA dashboard
articles.title: Service Incident - June 11, 2026 - AI Agents | All Pods - AIAA: Technical errors in AI agent conversations causing loss of access to AIAA dashboard
articles.body:
Summary

On June 11, 2026, between 16:23 UTC and 17:41 UTC, AIR/Premier customers in the US region experienced loss of access to the AI Agents Advanced Dashboard, with some end users encountering errors during AI agent conversations. The issue was caused by backend instability and was resolved after reverting to a previous system version.

Timeline

[EXAMPLE - to be copied from status.zendesk.com timeline]

June 11,2026 17:54 UTC | June 11, 2026 10:54 AM PT

Our engineers have identified the root cause of the issue impacting access to AI Agents dashboard, we have rolled back the offending change and this should both restore access to the dashboard, but also resolve the issue some end-users who experienced intermittent technical error replies during the incident. Our testing and metrics show complete recovery. We apologize for the inconvenience this issue caused.

Root Cause Analysis

The incident was caused by a deployment error that prevented the AI Agents dashboard backend from starting properly due to a missing configuration. This resulted in the service being unable to operate until the change was reversed.

Resolution

To resolve the issue, the deployment of the AI Agents dashboard backend was reverted to the previously stable version across all regions. This action stopped the system instability and restored access to dashboards and normal monitoring.

Remediation Items

  1. Document and clearly communicate that GCP deployments are prioritized first in the release sequence.
  2. Update deployment warnings to include clear guidance on monitoring expectations during and after releases.

FOR MORE INFORMATION

For current system status information about Zendesk and specific impacts to your account, visit our system status page. If you have additional questions about this incident, contact Zendesk customer support.

articles.name: What are the differences between the two Microsoft integrations for Zendesk?
articles.title: What are the differences between the two Microsoft integrations for Zendesk?
articles.body:
Differences between the Microsoft integrations in Zendesk

Zendesk offers two Microsoft integrations that can look similar at first glance. However, these apps are built for distinct Microsoft experiences and different support workflows.

  1. Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 focuses on support agents and broader support workflows within Microsoft Teams
  2. Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot focuses on users managing their own support requests within Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook

Experience differences between the two Microsoft integrations

The two integrations differ in where they work and how users access them.

Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 availability

  • Available options list only Microsoft Teams
  • Brings the Zendesk Agent Workspace into Teams
  • Users can work with Zendesk without leaving Teams

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot availability

  • Available in both Microsoft Teams and Outlook
  • Brings the Zendesk experience into Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook
  • Users can access the Zendesk agent from the Copilot agents list in Teams or Outlook
  • Users can work with Zendesk without leaving Teams or Outlook

Intended workflows and use case comparison

The two integrations also differ in the type of work they support.

Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 use cases

Use the Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 when you want a more complete Zendesk support workflow in Microsoft Teams only.

To perform user tasks:

  • Search and manage tickets
  • Create tickets
  • Add public or private comments
  • Browse help center articles
  • Manage IT assets
  • Work with views and macros
  • Look up users and organizations
  • Use plain language requests and slash commands

This integration is the better fit when you want a broader Zendesk support experience inside Teams and want the experience to respect Zendesk role permissions for different user types.

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot use cases

Use the Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot when you want Zendesk support capabilities inside Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook.

To perform user tasks:

  • Search help center articles
  • Create Zendesk tickets
  • Add public ticket comments
  • View tickets they created
  • Update tickets they created
  • Resolve tickets they created

This integration is the better fit when you want Zendesk support features embedded in Copilot and only need help center search and management of their own support requests.

Differences in setup and administration

The two integrations also differ in how admins set them up and how users access them.

Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 set up

  • Users install the app in Microsoft Teams
  • Users connect their Zendesk account using a /login command
  • The app supports /logout, /help, and /feedback
  • Access is governed by Zendesk permissions
  • The app is designed to be used directly by end users, agents, and admins depending on permissions

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot set up

  • Admins set up the integration in Admin Center
  • Admins connect the Microsoft account
  • Admins can turn on or turn off individual features
  • A Microsoft 365 admin must also allow users to install and use the Zendesk agent
  • Multiple Microsoft account connections are supported

articles.name: Why doesn’t my webhook payload include the URL behind a link in a ticket comment?
articles.title: Why doesn’t my webhook payload include the URL behind a link in a ticket comment?
articles.body:
Why link URLs aren't in my webhook payload

When the system sends comment placeholders to a webhook or URL target, the system renders the content as unformatted text instead of full HTML. Because of this, the webhook payload won't include hyperlinks that exist only as the underlying href of a button or formatted HTML link.

For example, a ticket comment can contain text or an image with a hyperlink, such as Click here.

When a webhook payload uses a comment placeholder, it includes only the visible text associated with an image or link, not the destination URL behind it. Any image URL that appears in the payload is typically the attachment or hosted image URL stored by the system, not the hyperlink target behind the image or button.

This happens when you use comment data placeholders such as:

  • ticket.description
  • ticket.latest_comment_html
  • ticket.latest_public_comment
  • ticket.latest_public_comment_html

Why does the link still work in the Zendesk UI?

The Zendesk ticket interface can render email or comment content to view, which includes clickable images, buttons, or formatted links. However, webhook placeholders don't return the same content as fully preserved HTML. Instead, they use a processed text version of the comment or description.

As a result, the behavior in the UI can differ from the content in the webhook payload.

Why do some links still appear in the payload?

Some links can still appear if they are present in the plain-text version of the email or comment.

For example, a webhook payload can include:

  • a raw URL written directly in the message
  • a link preserved in plain text or Markdown format
  • image or attachment links represented in a text-friendly format

By contrast, links embedded only inside more complex HTML structures, such as table-based buttons, won't be preserved in the webhook payload.

Is there a placeholder that reliably returns the hidden URL?

There's no placeholder that reliably extracts and returns the underlying hyperlink URL for button-style or HTML-formatted links in webhook payloads.

What can I do if I need the exact URL from the ticket comments?

If your workflow requires the actual hyperlink destination, consider one of these approaches:

  • Option 1: Extract the URL and store it in a custom ticket field. Use your own logic to parse the inbound email or comment, extract the URL, and write it into a custom ticket field. You can then use the custom ticket field placeholder to include that custom field in your webhook payload.

Option 2: Use the Ticket Comments API:

Do not rely on the webhook payload to contain the link. Instead, send the ticket ID in the webhook payload and have your external system call the Ticket Comments API to retrieve the comment content and parse the URL there.

This is generally the most reliable approach if you need to work with the actual hyperlink destination.

articles.name: Announcing audio playback for the email channel
articles.title: Announcing audio playback for the email channel
articles.body:
We’re excited to announce that our embedded audio player in Agent Workspace is now available for the email channel in addition to messaging conversations. This player allows agents to listen to audio messages without downloading files externally, providing a smoother and faster support experience.

This article includes these sections:

  • What is changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What is changing?

Agents can now listen to customer audio messages attached to email comments right inside their Zendesk interface with a simple play button. This improves workflow efficiency and reduces response time.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

We want to help agents provide faster, more accessible support. Many customers communicate using audio, especially those who rely on voice due to literacy or accessibility needs. This change enhances the ability to serve these customers effectively, improving satisfaction and productivity.

What do I need to do?

No action is required on your part to enable this feature. It will be automatically available in Agent Workspace. Just look for the new play button next to audio messages and enjoy the simpler way to listen and assist. See Playing back audio comments in email and messaging tickets.

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our Community forum  where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Announcing updates to knowledge sources and search rules in AI agents
articles.title: Announcing updates to knowledge sources and search rules in AI agents
articles.body:
Some important changes are coming to how knowledge sources and search rules work in AI agents. These changes affect only customers who bought the AI agents - Advanced add-on before March 10, 2026. Customers who got started with AI agents after that date already have knowledge sources and search rules that work in the current paradigm.

On March 10, 2026, Zendesk implemented some infrastructure changes that affected newly implementing customers only. Starting July 1, 2026, we’re implementing these same infrastructure changes for existing customers. This migration to the new infrastructure will take place over a period of time, ending on September 30, 2026.

This announcement answers the following questions:

  • What’s the impact on my account?
  • What’s changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?
  • How do I know when I’ll be migrated?

What’s the impact on my account?

Your account will be migrated during a period of low activity, as determined by an analysis of your account’s historic activity. During the migration, you can expect a downtime within the AI agents workspace only of up to two minutes.

During this two-minute downtime, AI agent channels will experience the following effects: 

  • Messaging: Ongoing messaging conversations may be interrupted, and new messages won’t be sent or received. 
    • In in-progress conversations, customers will see a message with a retry button. 
    • When the migration is complete, service will automatically resume and conversations can continue as normal. 
    • Messages sent on social channels will be delivered after the interruption.
  • Email: For emails sent by customers during the migration window, tickets are created but no AI agent response is sent. 
    • For the tickets created during the migration, you can view them and respond to them separately.
  • Voice: Active voice calls will be interrupted with a message informing the customer of needed maintenance, and the call ends.
    • Because a new ticket is created when a call starts, this information will be available in your account after the migration is complete.
    • After the migration, customers can start calls again.

Additionally, during the downtime, admins will see the following message when attempting to access any page in the AI agents workspace:

What’s changing?

Beyond the temporary effects during the downtime window, the ongoing noticeable effects of these infrastructure changes have to do with the way knowledge sources and search rules work in AI agents. This sections below walk through these effects specifically:

  • Zendesk help center connections consolidated and locale-scoping added to search rules
  • Zendesk help center as a knowledge source respects viewing permissions
  • Existing CSV imports work, but new imports cannot be added yet
  • Connecting to different Zendesk help centers is now possible
  • Zendesk web crawler replaces the previous web crawler
  • More built-in connectors to external content sources are available

Zendesk help center connections consolidated and locale-scoping added to search rules

If you previously imported the same Zendesk help center as a knowledge source multiple times (each with a different locale specified), we’ll consolidate that into a single knowledge source—the Zendesk help center belonging to the same brand as the AI agent. We’ll then update your search rules to preserve the functionality that you originally configured. This might mean that we add, update, or merge locale filters in your existing search rules. In cases where a search rule previously had URLs under the Match ANY conditions evaluator at the search rule level, they will be moved to the Includes any evaluator at the parameter level.

If you previously imported a Zendesk help center from another account (such as importing your production help center into your sandbox account), we’ll recreate these connections using the Zendesk knowledge connector.

Optional context on why this locale-scoping is necessary: In the new infrastructure, a brand contains a single help center that can serve articles in multiple locales. In the previous infrastructure, individual knowledge sources were locale-scoped by design. Each knowledge source connection pointed to a single locale's article set, identified by the locale included in the URL (for example, subdomain.zendesk.com/hc/en-us). As a result, a customer with a help center available in three locales (for example, en-us, ro, and da) may have configured only two knowledge base connections (en-us and ro), intentionally excluding the third. When migrating, all knowledge source connections for the same Zendesk help center are consolidated into a single connection for the brand associated with the AI agent. Without the explicit locale scoping we perform as part of the migration, the knowledge source connection in the new infrastructure would return articles from all three locales, including da, which the customer never configured. 

Zendesk help center as a knowledge source respects viewing permissions 

In the infrastructure prior to March 10, articles you’d imported into an AI agent from a Zendesk help center were either public or private to everyone.

Going forward, Zendesk help centers connected as a knowledge source in AI agents respect article view permissions. You can restrict articles to specific user segments, so certain articles appear only to groups you define, such as premium customers or specific regions. You can also create internal-only articles that only signed-in team members see. You no longer need to create search rules for these scenarios.

For more information, see Using restricted help center content in AI agent responses.

If you want to keep articles hidden from both customers and internal employees but still available to an AI agent, use the Zendesk connector. When your account moves to the new infrastructure, the Zendesk connector is set up for you automatically, so this content keeps working in your AI agent with no action needed from you. If you want your AI agent to respect article view permissions instead, connect your help center as a knowledge source and remove the Zendesk connector at any time.

Existing CSV imports work, but new imports cannot be added yet

If you’ve already imported a CSV file as a knowledge source, your AI agent will continue to be able to leverage that information. Your existing CSV content is migrated automatically to the new infrastructure and remains fully retrievable by your AI agents, with no action required on your part.

However, you cannot yet add new CSV content in the AI agents workspace. This includes uploading new versions of an existing CSV. To address this, we're building a native, self-service CSV importer, which we plan to announce in the future. In the meantime, if you need to import new CSV content, contact Zendesk customer support.

Connecting to different Zendesk help centers is now possible

As previously announced, you can connect additional Zendesk instances to your primary Zendesk instance to make its help center content available as a knowledge source. This means you can connect a sandbox environment AI agent to your production environment help center, for example.

For more information, see Connecting Zendesk as an external knowledge source.

Zendesk web crawler replaces the previous web crawler 

The Zendesk web crawler is replacing the previous version of the web crawler. The Zendesk web crawler supports a great majority of usage scenarios, including automatic link discovery and dynamically rendered content. After it’s set up, the crawler runs on a daily basis to ensure that data is always fresh.


If you’ve already imported content using the previous version of the web crawler as a knowledge source, your AI agent will continue to be able to leverage that information.

For customers who need additional controls over their web crawler setup, we’re planning the following future improvements:

  • Custom URL filters (include/exclude URL)
  • Increase number of base URLs
  • Residential proxy
  • Locale filtering and records multiplication
  • Custom HTML settings (remove/keep HTML elements)
  • Custom crawl scheduler
  • Click elements (including dynamic wait, sticky container, wait for selector, and soft wait for selector)
  • Support subdomains and other domains

More built-in connectors to external content sources are available

In the new infrastructure, Zendesk offers a wide array of connectors to external knowledge sources, including Google Docs, Sharepoint, Confluence and more. For the current full list, see Viewing all available Knowledge connectors.

Note that native connectors to Salesforce and Freshdesk are not currently available, but are planned for the future. In the meantime, if you need to import new content from these sources, contact Zendesk customer support.

We’re committed to continuing to increase the external content connector offering for our customers. 

Why is Zendesk making this change?

Zendesk is making this change to simplify setup of search rules and knowledge sources. Specifically, our goals with this change are:

  • Everything works out of the box. In the new infrastructure, your search rules, article view permissions, and knowledge sources are configured to work immediately. You don't need to set up CRM integrations or manage API tokens to get started. Permissions work right away without waiting hours for content to be re-indexed or processed.
  • Merging Zendesk knowledge connections and search rules preserves what you've built. If you've tuned search rules for each language, we want those rules to work the same way after migration. We handle the merging, but you'll want to validate it works as expected.
  • User segments give you visibility control. You can now segment your audience and show different articles to different customer groups in AI agent responses, giving you more flexibility in how you organize your help center.
  • More ways to bring in your content. Your knowledge can live anywhere: a Zendesk help center, an external system, a website, or a CSV file. Many sources connect natively today, including help centers, sandboxes, web-crawled websites, and external systems like Google Docs, SharePoint, and Confluence. Others are on the way, including native Salesforce and Freshdesk connectors and a self-service CSV importer. Until then, our team can help you bring in CSV and other content directly.

What do I need to do?

We're handling the technical migration, so you don’t need to do anything to request the kickoff of the process. If you had the same Zendesk help center connected multiple times, the connections are automatically consolidated and the search rules are updated so that your single knowledge source serves all languages correctly. Your article visibility settings move over, and any previously imported CSV content becomes available with user segment controls.

We test everything before it goes to you, but migration is still a big change. That's why we want you to validate that things work the way you expect. Specifically, you should perform the following tasks:

  • Review your article visibility strategy. Think about whether user segments make sense for your help center. If you want to restrict articles to specific customer groups or create internal-only content, now's the time to plan that out. You don't have to implement it right away, but it's worth considering.
  • Check your CSV content. Your existing CSV content is automatically carried over as part of the migration. After the migration, confirm your AI agent still retrieves this content as expected. If you need to add new CSV content before the self-service CSV importer is released, contact Zendesk customer support.

Finally, here are some additional resources that might be helpful as you acclimate to the new infrastructure: 

  • Connecting knowledge sources to power generative replies in AI agents
  • Configuring search rules for knowledge sources for AI agents

How do I know when I’ll be migrated?

The migration period runs from July 1 to September 30, 2026. Your account will be scheduled for migration at some point during this time. Prior to your account’s migration, admins and account owners will see an in-product message that appears at the top of the page.

Additionally, you can verify whether your account is on the previous infrastructure or the new infrastructure by checking the URL when you access the AI agent workspace. If the URL includes:

  • *.ultimate.ai, you’re on the old infrastructure.
  • <subdomain>.zendesk.com, you’re on the new infrastructure.

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our community forum where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk customer support.

articles.name: Error: Some Talk features aren't available right now
articles.title: Error: Some Talk features aren't available right now
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

My agents can make and receive calls, but an error appears. Some Talk features aren’t available right now. You can still make and receive calls. Multiple features in Talk don't work as expected:

  • The call generated a ticket, but that ticket doesn't pop up automatically in the Agent Workspace
  • If wrap-up is active, the user gets dropped from the call
  • The pause and transfer buttons aren't visible in the call console
  • Even if the option Allow agents to control call recording is turned on in Admin Center, the call record button isn't visible in the call console

Resolution steps

This message indicates that Talk encountered poor network conditions and operates in an unstable network mode. This mode prioritizes only essential call functions so that your agents can continue to take calls from your customers. When network conditions improve, Unstable Network Mode automatically disengages and full call functionality resumes for your agents.

If your agents encounter Unstable Network Mode often, their browser or computer may not be able to connect to the required Zendesk Talk URLs. If you don't allow this communication, your agents can perform only the accept, decline, and hang up actions.

To resolve this issue:

  1. Contact your network administrator to activate communication with your computer or browser. If your company already permits all AWS IP ranges, you don't need to add the required URLs for Talk. If the required URLs or their IP ranges have been added to your company network configuration, ensure that the connections are allowed in your VPN, firewall, and any antivirus software you use.
  2. Refresh your browser tab. The error message may appear due to poor internet connectivity.
  3. If you open multiple browser tabs when you accept or make Talk calls, reduce them to fewer than 4 to see if that reduces the frequency of the issue

See these articles for more information:

  • How can I troubleshoot Talk issues?
  • What are the most common Talk error messages?

articles.name: Workflow: Closing a large number of tickets
articles.title: Workflow: Closing a large number of tickets
articles.body:
Use an automation or the application programming interface (API) to close tickets in bulk. This archives the tickets and removes them from your views.

This article covers these sections:

  • Close tickets with the API
  • Close tickets with an automation

Close tickets with the API

Use the API to close a large group of tickets. You can use the Advanced Data Privacy and Protection (ADPP) add-on or the ticket API endpoint.

Create a deletion schedule for advanced data retention policies

With the advanced data retention policies of the ADPP add-on, you can create a deletion schedule based on your ticket's Last Updated date and Brand. After you activate a schedule, it searches for and deletes tickets or end users that meet the conditions.

Use the API to bulk close tickets

Use the ticket API endpoint to find and manage tickets. For more information about the API, see Admin's guide to the Zendesk API.

If you're new to APIs, use Postman to send a request to your Zendesk Support account. For steps to use Postman to make API requests to your Zendesk account, see How do I set up Postman to make API requests to my Zendesk account?.

Create an automation to set ticket status to closed

Use an automation to create a time-based rule that closes your tickets.

To create this automation:

  1. Add a new automation
  2. Name the new automation
  3. Add these conditions under Meet all of the following conditions:
    • Ticket: Hours since update > Greater than > (Specify the time here). If the tickets you want to close have a specific status, use other conditions such as Ticket: Hours since open, Ticket: Hours since pending, and Ticket: Hours since solved.
    • Ticket: Status category > Less than > Closed
  4. Under Perform these actions, select Ticket: Status category: Closed
  5. Click Create automation.
Note: In accounts with custom ticket statuses deactivated, use the condition Ticket: Status instead of Ticket: Status category. For the actions, use the action Ticket: Status | Closed.

articles.name: Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?
articles.title: Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?
articles.body:
Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?

Yes. Agents can view all tickets by default unless you configure otherwise. You can use one Support account for both customer-facing agents and internal agents. Consider privacy risks, especially if customer-facing agents submit tickets to internal teams as requesters. Change their role permissions to prevent agents from accessing tickets in certain groups.

  • Restrict agents to groups
  • Pros and cons of a single Support account
  • Pros and cons of separate Support accounts

Restrict agents to groups

On the Enterprise plans, designate a group as private to ensure that agents outside the group cannot access tickets assigned to it.

Agents who do not have access to a ticket in a private group cannot view it or search for it. You can also prevent the agent requester, as an end user, from seeing private comments or agent-only fields on the ticket.

If your plan does not include private groups, add agents to groups and restrict them to tickets in their groups from the custom role or the user profile, depending on your plan level.

For more information on restricting agent ticket access to groups, see Creating private ticket groups and granting agents access.

Role and group restrictions have these limits:

  • If an agent must access tickets across multiple groups, the agent must belong to all necessary groups. When an agent reassigns a ticket outside those groups, the agent loses access to the ticket.
  • If you add an agent as a CC on a ticket, that agent gains access in the Support interface, even if the CC’d agent does not belong to the ticket group.
  • If an agent requests a ticket in an Human Resources (HR) group, the agent can see all ticket fields and internal comments. This can be a problem when the HR agent must add sensitive details about the requester.

Pros of a single Support account

  • One interface to manage all groups and departments
  • Single sign-on (SSO) configuration is easier
  • Some existing business rules, apps, and administrative settings can be reused
  • Centralized reporting for all ticket activity
  • Consolidate internal knowledge within one help center
  • You can enable agents to manage and submit requests from the help center to simulate parts of the end user experience

Cons of a single Support account

  • If you’re on the Professional, Growth, or Team plan, all agents can see internal comments and agent-only ticket fields. If an agent submits a ticket to HR, the agent can see all fields and internal comments on their own ticket
  • If you add a restricted agent as a CC or follower on a ticket, that agent gains access to that ticket regardless of group membership
  • All admins have access to all data within the HR account
  • Maintenance of groups and custom role restrictions can become unwieldy
  • Custom roles cannot be nested
  • To let non-HR agents see all tickets except HR tickets, your setup may require multiple groups
  • The first reply time service level agreement (SLA) does not apply to tickets that non-HR agents create

Pros of separate Support accounts

  • Non-HR agents don’t have access to agent-only ticket fields and internal comments in the HR account
  • Sensitive data stays private when the HR agent uses an internal note
  • HR and non-HR workflows remain completely separate
  • You have more flexibility for custom role permissions in both accounts, rather than requiring the All within their groups ticket access permission

Cons of separate Support accounts

  • This option requires two accounts
  • Single sign-on (SSO) maintenance can be more complex because your SSO platform must connect to multiple instances. In account A, a user might be an agent, but in account B the same user might be an end user
  • Some integrations may not function in multiple accounts. For example, a Jira integration cannot integrate with two separate accounts
  • End users must use separate help centers to manage their requests and view content. Some training may be required to help users differentiate between the two help centers if they use both accounts
